note_data.title: Thermodynamics
keyPoints: One radian is defined as the angle in the sector of a circle when the arc length equals the radius of the circle
Angular speed ω = v/r = 2π/T = 2πf, where v is linear speed, r is radius, T is time period, and f is frequency
An object in uniform circular motion has constantly changing velocity due to changing direction, requiring centripetal acceleration
Centripetal force always acts perpendicular to motion towards the center of the circle
Centripetal acceleration a = v²/r = ω²r, and centripetal force F = mv²/r = mω²r
Gravitational fields are areas where objects with mass experience non-contact forces
Newton's law of gravitation: F = Gm₁m₂/r², where force is proportional to product of masses and inversely proportional to square of distance
Gravitational field strength g = F/m in uniform fields, and g = GM/r² in radial fields

definitions.definition: The angle in a sector of a circle when the arc length equals the radius of the circle
The angle turned through by an object in any given direction in radians
The angle an object moves through per unit time
The acceleration experienced by an object moving in a circular path at constant speed, always directed toward the center
The force that acts perpendicular to motion toward the center of the circle, causing circular motion
An area where objects with mass experience a non-contact force
The force per unit mass exerted by a gravitational field on an object

howToAnswer.instruction: Give the law or definition in one clear sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the physical reason using 'because' - link energy changes to molecular behavior
Show all steps clearly. State the formula, substitute values, give final answer with correct units
Draw labeled axes, show correct shape of curve, mark key points and values

modelAnswer.question: A gas undergoes an isothermal expansion from volume 2.0 × 10⁻³ m³ to 6.0 × 10⁻³ m³. During this process, 450 J of heat is supplied to the gas. Calculate the work done by the gas and explain the change in internal energy.
modelAnswer.answer: For an isothermal process, temperature is constant, so change in internal energy ΔU = 0. Using first law: ΔU = Q + W, therefore 0 = 450 + W, so W = -450 J. The negative sign shows work is done by the gas (expansion). Since temperature is constant for an ideal gas, there is no change in internal energy because internal energy depends only on temperature.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Correct application of first law ΔU = Q + W
Mark 3: W = -450 J with correct sign
Mark 4: Explanation that internal energy unchanged because temperature constant

commonMistakes: Confusing degrees with radians - remember to convert using π/180
Thinking that uniform circular motion has zero acceleration because speed is constant
Forgetting that centripetal force always points toward the center, not in the direction of motion
Assuming gravitational field strength is constant everywhere - it varies in radial fields
Not recognizing that uniform spheres act as point masses at their center for external calculations
examinerTips: Remember the inverse square relationship for gravitational field strength - if distance doubles, field strength decreases by factor of 4
Use the correct formula for centripetal force depending on given quantities: F = mv²/r when given speed and radius, F = mω²r when given angular speed
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: P-V diagram showing isothermal (curved line), adiabatic (steeper curve), and constant pressure/volume processes. Work done equals area under curve. Label axes as pressure (Pa) vs volume (m³).
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain the first law of thermodynamics and calculate work done during an isothermal expansion of an ideal gas
O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question testing understanding of adiabatic processes and which thermodynamic quantity remains constant
M/J 2022 P2 Q3b (4 marks) — candidates had to draw and explain a P-V diagram for a complete thermodynamic cycle and calculate net work done
O/N 2021 P2 Q5 (8 marks) — comprehensive question on heat engines requiring calculation of efficiency and explanation of why 100% efficiency is impossible
M/J 2021 P1 Q18 (1 mark) — tested knowledge of specific heat capacities and the relationship between internal energy and temperature for ideal gases

workedExample.question: An ideal gas undergoes an isothermal expansion at 300K. The initial volume is 2.0 × 10⁻³ m³ and final volume is 6.0 × 10⁻³ m³. If the gas contains 0.50 mol, calculate the work done by the gas. (R = 8.31 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹) [4 marks]
steps: Step 1: Identify the process type - isothermal expansion means temperature T remains constant at 300K
Step 2: Use the formula for work done in isothermal process: W = nRT ln(Vf/Vi)
workedExample.answer: The work done by the gas is 1370 J (or 1.37 kJ)
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for identifying isothermal process, 1 mark for correct formula, 1 mark for correct substitution, 1 mark for final answer with appropriate significant figures

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused work done BY the gas with work done ON the gas' — always check the sign convention and specify direction when calculating work in thermodynamic processes

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to convert units or used incorrect gas constant values' — always verify that pressure is in Pa, volume in m³, and use R = 8.31 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ for calculations

Examiner Report 2021: 'Common error was stating that adiabatic means no temperature change' — remember that adiabatic means no heat transfer (Q = 0), while isothermal means no temperature change (ΔT = 0)
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw P-V diagrams showing different thermodynamic processes: isothermal curves (hyperbolic shape, PV = constant), adiabatic curves (steeper than isothermal, PVᵞ = constant), isochoric lines (vertical lines at constant volume), and isobaric lines (horizontal lines at constant pressure). Include arrows showing direction of process, label axes clearly with units, and shade areas under curves to represent work done. For cyclic processes, show the complete loop with clockwise direction for heat engines.
note_data.quickRecap: Circular motion involves angular displacement measured in radians and requires centripetal force directed toward the center. Objects experience centripetal acceleration even at constant speed due to changing direction. Gravitational fields exert forces on masses, with strength following Newton's law of gravitation and inverse square relationships in radial fields.
